title: CXMT's $8.6B IPO Tests the Limits of Blacklisting, Apple Knocks on Blacklisted Doors, and White House Launches Gold Eagle
subhead: China's DRAM champion CXMT doubles its IPO target to $8.6B while Apple lobbies to buy from Pentagon-blacklisted chipmakers — exposing the gap between export controls and supply reality. Meanwhile, the White House launches an AI-powered cybersecurity clearinghouse, and ZTE wins access to Nvidia H200 chips despite its national-security designation.

sections.heading: CXMT Doubles IPO to $8.6B as China's DRAM Champion Goes Public
sections.body:
ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT), China's leading DRAM maker, has set its IPO fundraising target at 57.9 billion yuan ($8.6 billion), nearly doubling its earlier target of 29.5 billion yuan. The offering on Shanghai's Nasdaq-style STAR Market is set to begin trading on July 27, making it the largest semiconductor IPO in Chinese history and the biggest IPO in Asia so far in 2026, according to Reuters.

The company's revenue more than doubled to CNY 55 billion (~$8 billion) in 2025, driven almost entirely by surging AI infrastructure and data center demand. CXMT was founded in 2016 by Zhu Yiming and has grown with state backing into a national champion for China's push to reduce memory chip dependence on Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron. But CXMT also sits on the Pentagon's blacklist of Chinese military-linked companies — and on the Commerce Department's list of over 100 entities approved for blacklisting that remain unpublished.

The IPO's scale signals that Chinese investors are betting on CXMT's valuation soaring as DRAM demand outstrips global supply. Reuters Breakingviews notes that for years the West prioritized investing in advanced silicon, but Chinese giants like CXMT 'will seize market share' in the low-end and mid-range memory chips that still power most of the world's devices.
sections.whyItMatters: CXMT's mega-IPO is happening while the company is simultaneously blacklisted by the US Pentagon. That tension — between Western export controls and Chinese firms' ability to raise billions domestically — shows that sanctions don't stop growth; they redirect it. CXMT is filling the DRAM gap created by the very supply crunch that AI demand has produced.

sections.heading: Apple Seeks Chips from Pentagon-Blacklisted Firms as Memory Shortage Bites
sections.body:
Apple CEO Tim Cook has personally lobbied the Trump administration for permission to buy memory chips from CXMT and YMTC (Yangtze Memory Technologies Co.), two Chinese manufacturers on the Pentagon's blacklist, according to CryptoBriefing. The world's most valuable company is negotiating with firms the US government has designated as security threats — exposing just how severe the global memory shortage has become.

The global DRAM market is in the grip of a supply-demand imbalance that analysts expect to persist until at least 2027. Prices have surged 98% amid AI-driven demand, and the shortage extends beyond cutting-edge AI chips into the commodity memory that powers phones, laptops, and servers. Apple's willingness to seek exemptions for blacklisted suppliers indicates that even the most powerful tech companies cannot find adequate alternatives.

YMTC was added to the Entity List in late 2022. CXMT has been on the Pentagon's Chinese military companies list, and the Commerce Department has approved but not published its addition to the Entity List. Apple's request forces a policy question: if the world's biggest chip buyers cannot source memory without turning to sanctioned firms, either the sanctions regime or the supply chain needs to change.
sections.whyItMatters: Apple lobbying to buy from blacklisted companies isn't a niche procurement problem — it's evidence that the global memory chip supply chain cannot function without Chinese producers. The West invested in frontier chips; China invested in volume. Now that asymmetry is creating pressure at exactly the point where sanctions are supposed to bite.

sections.heading: ZTE Gets H200 Access as Congress Grills Commerce on 'Trivial' Shipments
sections.body:
Commerce Under Secretary Jeffrey Kessler told the House Foreign Affairs Committee on July 14 that Nvidia H200 chip shipments to China remain 'trivial' despite $10 billion in approved export licenses. Kessler's testimony was the first official confirmation that any H200s have shipped to China, but he emphasized the volumes are negligible.

On the same day Kessler testified, the Commerce Department added new names to its approved buyer list — including ZTE Kangxun Telecom, a unit of ZTE Corp, which the US government has designated as a national security threat to federal agencies under the 2018 NDAA. Server manufacturer Maginfra and a Kingsoft Cloud subsidiary (approved for AMD chips) also received clearances, as reported by Reuters and eWeek.

Chinese firms have collectively ordered more than 2 million H200 chips for 2026, against Nvidia's total inventory of roughly 700,000 units. Beijing has also restricted H200 use to AI model training only, directing companies to use domestic processors — primarily Huawei's Ascend line — for inference workloads. One proposal would require each H200 purchase to be bundled with a set ratio of Chinese-made chips, according to the South China Morning Post.

Separately, over 100 Chinese entities approved for Commerce Department blacklisting — including DeepSeek and CXMT — remain unpublished on the Entity List, a gap that drew bipartisan criticism at the hearing.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between license approvals and actual chip deliveries is the story. $10 billion in licenses means nothing if almost no chips arrive. But approving ZTE — a firm the US itself calls a security threat — to buy H200s shows how the licensing process is outpacing the enforcement logic. Meanwhile, 100+ blacklisted entities sit in bureaucratic limbo.

sections.heading: White House Launches Gold Eagle, an AI-Powered Cybersecurity Clearinghouse
sections.body:
The White House on July 14 announced Gold Eagle, a new federal cybersecurity clearinghouse designed to use AI to identify, prioritize, and patch software vulnerabilities before attackers can exploit them. The program connects officials from the White House, Treasury, DHS, CISA, and the Defense Department with private companies and open-source software groups.

Gold Eagle has already started receiving vulnerability reports and sending repair guidance, according to the White House. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the government would work 'hand in hand with the private sector,' while Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth called for a 'wartime footing to the cyber domain,' Forbes reported.

The initiative responds to a worsening speed problem. Verizon's 2026 Data Breach Investigations Report found that 31% of breaches began with vulnerability exploitation — surpassing stolen credentials for the first time. Mandiant's 2026 M-Trends report found that the median time for stolen access to pass from one criminal group to another dropped from 8+ hours in 2022 to 22 seconds in 2025. The program also responds to the rise of AI-powered ransomware like JadePuffer, which uses agentic models to handle much of the intrusion process autonomously.

However, key details remain unresolved. The White House has not named participating companies, published patching speed targets, or explained how severity disputes will be resolved. Europe's parallel approach — the Cyber Resilience Act, requiring manufacturers to report exploited vulnerabilities starting September 2026 — relies on legal mandates rather than voluntary cooperation.
sections.whyItMatters: Gold Eagle is Washington's answer to the fact that vulnerability exploitation has become the #1 breach vector and that criminal access changes hands in seconds. But voluntary coordination without enforceable timelines or named participants is a partial answer. The contrast with Europe's regulation-first approach will reveal which model actually produces results.

sections.heading: US Sanctions First VPN Service, UK and EU Target 24 Russian Cyber Actors
sections.body:
The US Treasury's OFAC on Tuesday sanctioned First VPN Service (1VPNS) — marking the first time the US has sanctioned a VPN provider — along with its Ukrainian administrator Dmytro Rashevskyi and Belarusian national Yegeniy Silayev, who sold cryptors to conceal ransomware. The VPN, operational since 2014, had been dismantled in a joint law enforcement operation in May 2026 for enabling ransomware attacks on US businesses, hospitals, and municipal governments, The Hacker News reported.

Simultaneously, the UK and EU sanctioned 24 Russian individuals and entities for 'persistent and increasingly reckless' cyber operations, including GRU Unit 29155's cyber division and FSB Centre 16. The sanctions target operators behind proxy networks, the Lumma Stealer infostealer, and destructive sabotage operations against Poland's energy grid in late 2025.

The FBI and CISA also issued a new advisory on FSB Centre 16's exploitation of poorly configured routers worldwide to hack into critical infrastructure networks. The actors scan for SNMP-enabled devices with default community strings, copy configurations, and transfer them to attacker-controlled servers — a technique exploiting vulnerabilities dating back to CVE-2008-4128 and CVE-2018-0171 in Cisco devices.

On the same day, the DOJ unsealed a 2024 indictment charging three Russian nationals and two companies (ML.Cloud and Media Land) for running bulletproof hosting services that enabled phishing, DDoS attacks, ransomware, and cybercrime marketplaces targeting at least 42 entities across 21 US states, causing tens of millions in losses. The US is offering a $10 million reward for information on the operators.
sections.whyItMatters: Sanctioning a VPN service is a new tool in the cyber enforcement toolkit — it signals that infrastructure providers are no longer invisible. The coordinated Western action against Russian cyber actors (including the GRU and FSB) is broad, but the underlying problem hasn't changed: Russia's cyber ecosystem blends state and criminal actors so thoroughly that sanctions hit proxies while the structures persist.

sections.heading: UAE Rewarded with Unrestricted AI Chip Access After Iran War Support
sections.body:
The Trump administration reclassified the UAE into the same export control tier as European nations, South Korea, and India, lifting previous restrictions that required Commerce Department licenses for advanced chip purchases. Under a rule announced July 11, UAE companies — including G42 — can now buy Nvidia chips without restriction for at least nine months, the New York Post reported. The move also lifts export hurdles for Microsoft and OpenAI, both of which have announced data center plans in the UAE.

The reclassification follows the UAE's military role in the Iran conflict, where it carried out dozens of airstrikes, intercepted hundreds of missiles, and helped keep oil flowing through the Strait of Hormuz. One US official said that support 'convinced the White House and senior administration officials that the UAE had proven itself to be a dependable ally.'

The decision faces political scrutiny because the UAE's national security adviser, Sheikh Tahnoon bin Zayed Al Nahyan — who controls G42 — became a major business partner of the Trump family last year through a $500 million stake in World Liberty Financial. Rep. Sydney Kamlager-Dove (D-CA) called it a potential 'illegal pay-to-play scheme' during a House committee hearing. Commerce official Jeffrey Kessler said he never discussed AI chip exports to the UAE with Trump family members or World Liberty Financial executives.

Hudson Institute senior fellow Michael Sobolik noted that while the UAE has been a great partner, 'that doesn't necessarily mean they've demonstrated the capability to keep a data center secure.'
sections.whyItMatters: AI chips are now explicit currency in security bargains. The UAE traded military support for compute access, and the deal is worth billions. But the speed of the reclassification — and the Trump family's business ties to G42's owner — raise questions about whether security safeguards or commercial relationships are driving export policy.

sections.heading: ASML Raises 2026 Guidance as AI Chip Demand Continues to Surge
sections.body:
ASML, the world's only manufacturer of extreme ultraviolet lithography machines, raised its 2026 net sales guidance to €36-40 billion, up from its previous range, after posting Q2 revenue of €9.3 billion and net profit of €2.9 billion, beating analyst estimates. The Dutch company also lifted its gross margin outlook to 54-56%.

The revised guidance reflects sustained demand for advanced chipmaking tools as AI spending accelerates. ASML's high-NA EUV machines command prices approaching $400 million each, and every chip below the 5nm process node requires EUV lithography. The company is expanding chipmaking capacity to meet order volumes that continue to exceed expectations.

ASML's guidance upgrade is a leading indicator for the entire semiconductor supply chain. When the company that makes the machines that make the chips raises its forecast, it signals that chipmakers themselves are seeing enough demand to justify billions in capital expenditure. Singapore's May exports surging 38% on AI-related demand — as reported in today's headlines — corroborates the picture from the demand side.
sections.whyItMatters: ASML's upward revision is the strongest signal yet that AI chip demand is not a bubble — chipmakers are investing billions in tooling because their customers are placing real orders. The €36-40 billion range means ASML itself expects to sell significantly more EUV equipment than it forecast just months ago.

bottomLine: Today's developments expose the central contradiction in Western tech policy: the harder the US squeezes export controls, the more pressure builds at the seams. CXMT is raising $8.6B while blacklisted. Apple is lobbying to buy from blacklisted firms. ZTE — itself a designated security threat — now has H200 access. The UAE traded military support for unrestricted chip imports. And $10 billion in approved H200 licenses have produced only 'trivial' shipments. Meanwhile, the White House launched Gold Eagle to address the reality that vulnerability exploitation now outpaces human response times — with criminal access passing between groups in 22 seconds. The throughline: the gap between policy intent and operational reality keeps widening, whether the domain is chips, cyber, or orbital mirrors.
sources: Reuters, CryptoBriefing, SCMP, TechTimes, Forbes, The Hacker News, SecurityWeek, New York Post, eWeek, NL Times, DevDiscourse, AHA, Yahoo Finance, Ars Technica, Data Center Dynamics, Invezz
